name: Therattipal

steps.instruction: Pour 8 cups milk into a wide, heavy-bottomed vessel. Heat on medium flame.
Let the milk come to a boil, then reduce to a simmer.
To prevent milk from boiling over, place a small stainless steel plate inside the vessel.
Let the milk reduce slowly, stirring frequently (every 5 minutes) to prevent sticking.
After 2 to 3 hours, the milk will reduce to a mud-like consistency — about ¼ of the original volume.
Add 1 cup sugar and stir well. The mixture will liquefy slightly.
Continue cooking until it comes to a semi-solid, thick consistency.
Switch off when still slightly runny — it will solidify as it cools.
Add cardamom powder, mix well and let cool.
To a kadai, add 1 tin condensed milk and ½ cup milk. Keep flame on low.
Mix well until blended. Let it start to boil.
Once boiling, add ¼ cup fresh curd. The mixture will start to curdle, giving a grainy texture.
Cook on low flame, stirring continuously to avoid burning.
As it thickens, add 1 tbsp ghee. Keep scraping milk solids from the sides.
Add another tbsp ghee if needed. Cook until the colour turns golden.
The mixture will leave the sides of the kadai and come together like a mass.
Switch off when thick but still slightly flowing — it thickens further on cooling.
Cool for a few minutes and serve.

tips: Use a wide, heavy-bottomed pan for the traditional method to prevent burning and sticking.
Whole milk gives the richest flavour, but 2% milk also works — it just takes longer to reduce.
Raw sugar gives a more golden colour compared to white sugar.
Do not let the mixture dry out completely — switch off when still slightly goey.
For the quick method, use fresh curd that is not sour, or the sweet will taste tangy.
Ghee adds richness and prevents the therattipal from sticking to the pan.
You can slightly grease the pan with water before starting to prevent sticking.
Therattipal stays good for 3 to 4 days at room temperature and up to a week refrigerated.
